criteria: The agent has read Section 3 (Background) completely to understand the GPT architecture, attention mechanism notation (Q, K, V, Wo matrices, heads, forward attention A), and the VJP formulation used throughout the paper.
The agent has read Section 4 (Attention Layers Gradients) completely to understand the mathematical derivation of VJPs for Wo, Wv, Wq, Wk and the softmax derivative R.
The agent has read Section 5 (Reversed Attention) completely to understand the definition and properties of Reversed Attention (RA) as an implicit attention matrix analogous to the forward attention.
The agent has read Section 5.2 completely to understand the perturbation test setup: head masking/unmasking, AUC metric, comparison methods (RA, FA, CM, Random), tasks, and models.
The agent has read Section 5.3 completely to understand the attention patching procedure: collecting RA from training examples, averaging per head, and injecting into the forward pass attention maps.

The agent has read Appendix B to understand the full perturbation test details: ICL task format (Q/A n-shot prompts), the two CM implementations (CM1: zero-out, CM2: average activation from Todd et al.), and the use of GPT-j and OPT models.
The plan includes implementing the full GPT forward pass with correct attention head splitting and multi-head attention, producing per-head Q, K, V, A matrices as defined in Equations 1 and 2.

The plan includes computing the Reversed Attention matrix R following Equations 14-17, using the backward pass error signal from Wo, multiplied by V, and normalized via element-wise product with forward attention A.
The plan includes using RA (R) to compute VJPs for Wq (Equation 18) and Wk (Equation 19) by multiplying the RA matrix with forward pass keys K and queries Q respectively.

The plan includes implementing the perturbation test: masking all attention heads, unmasking them one at a time according to different orderings (RA norm, FA norm, CM, Random), measuring model accuracy at each step, and computing AUC.
The plan includes using GPT2-xl as the primary model for the ICL perturbation test and Llama2-7B for the natural language perturbation test.

The plan includes implementing the attention patching method: collecting RA maps from training examples without updating weights, averaging them per attention head, and adding them (with a scaling factor) to the forward pass attention maps during inference on test examples.
The plan includes evaluating attention patching on ICL tasks from Todd et al. (2023) with varying numbers of shots (N=0, 1, 5, 10) using GPT2-xl and OPT-1.3B.

The code correctly implements the forward pass attention computation for a single head as A^l = softmax(Q^l K^l^T / sqrt(d/h) + M), including the causal masking matrix M that sets upper-triangular entries to -inf.
The code correctly computes the intermediate error signal e^l = delta_o^l * Wo^T (Equation 12) from the VJP of the output projection matrix Wo for each token l.
The code correctly computes the VJP for the value matrix Wv as delta_v^j = sum_{l>=j} A_{l,j} * e^l (Equation 13), a weighted sum of error signals weighted by forward attention scores.
The code correctly computes the intermediate matrix E_tilde = Delta * Wo^T * V^T (Equation 16), representing a score between each token's error signal and each forward pass value vector.

The code correctly computes the full Reversed Attention matrix R = A odot (E_tilde^T - diag(A * E_tilde^T))^T * sqrt(h/d) (Equation 17), using the element-wise (Hadamard) product with the forward attention A to obtain the lower-triangular RA matrix.

The code correctly computes the VJP for the query matrix Wq as delta_q^j = R_j * K (Equation 18), where R_j is the j-th row of the Reversed Attention matrix and K contains all forward pass key vectors.

The code correctly computes the VJP for the key matrix Wk as delta_k^j = R_j^T * Q (Equation 19), where R_j^T is the j-th column of R and Q contains forward pass query vectors from all subsequent tokens.
The code loads GPT2-xl via HuggingFace Transformers and correctly hooks into or extracts the per-head attention matrices (Q, K, V, A, Wo outputs) needed to compute RA during a forward+backward pass.
The code loads Llama2-7B for the natural language perturbation test and correctly adapts the RA computation to this model architecture.
The code loads OPT-1.3B (and optionally GPT-j and OPT-350m) and correctly adapts the RA computation to these model architectures.
The code computes the norm of the RA map per attention head and uses it to rank heads from highest to lowest norm, enabling head-importance ordering for the perturbation test.
The code implements head masking (zeroing out the output of a given attention head) and supports the progressive unmasking procedure used in the perturbation test.

The code implements Causal Mediation (CM1): for each attention head, patch its output to zero and measure the indirect effect on the model's output probability for the target token, averaging over training examples to rank heads.
The code implements the second CM variant (CM2) from Todd et al. (2023) that patches each head's activation with the average activation collected from held-out examples instead of zeroing it out.

The code computes the AUC (Area Under the Curve) of model accuracy as a function of the percentage of unmasked attention heads for each head-ordering method, enabling comparison of RA, FA, CM, and Random.

The code loads the 21 ICL tasks from Todd et al. (2023) and formats them as n-shot prompts using the template 'Q: <question> 
A: <answer> 
...Q: <question> 
A:' with configurable shot counts (1-shot and 5-shot at minimum).
The code loads the 6 natural language relation tasks from Hernandez et al. (2024) using explicit sentence templates (e.g., 'The capital city of <X> is [Y]').

The code implements the attention patching procedure: performs forward and backward passes on each training example to collect per-head RA maps without modifying model weights, then averages the RA maps across training examples for each head.

The code injects the averaged RA map into the forward pass attention map of each corresponding attention head during test inference (adding RA scaled by a learning rate factor to the forward attention logits before softmax).
The code implements the FA (forward attention) patching baseline using the same procedure as RA patching but averaging forward attention maps from training examples instead of RA maps.
The attention patching implementation enforces that all training and test examples have the same token length and format, as required by the paper for consistent attention matrix sizes.
The perturbation test code is executed successfully on GPT2-xl for all 15 ICL tasks with both 1-shot and 5-shot prompting, and the AUC results for RA, FA, CM, and Random are computed without errors.
The perturbation test code is executed successfully on GPT2-xl for the 6 natural language tasks, producing AUC scores for RA, FA, CM, and Random ordering.
The perturbation test code is executed successfully on Llama2-7B for the natural language tasks, producing AUC scores comparable to those in Table 2.

The attention patching experiment is executed successfully on GPT2-xl for the ICL tasks (antonym, capitalize, choose-middle-of-3, next-item) with N=0, 1, 5, 10 shots, producing accuracy results without errors.
The attention patching experiment is executed successfully on OPT-1.3B for the same ICL tasks and shot counts as GPT2-xl, producing accuracy results without errors.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: RA consistently outperforms FA and Random in AUC for most ICL tasks with 5-shot prompting on GPT2-xl (e.g., RA=0.32 vs FA=0.05 for antonym, 5-shot; RA=0.44 vs FA=0.09 for person-sport, 5-shot).

The execution outcomes match Table 2: RA achieves competitive or better AUC than CM on country-capital and person-plays-pro-sport tasks in Llama2-7B (RA=0.43 and 0.48 respectively vs CM=0.07 and 0.39).

The execution outcomes match Table 3: RA patching consistently outperforms FA patching across tasks and shot counts for both GPT2-xl and OPT-1.3B (e.g., RA=0.56 vs FA=0.43 for antonym with N=1 on GPT2-xl; RA=1.00 vs FA=0.50 for capitalize with N=1 on GPT2-xl).

The execution outcomes show that RA maps are visually sparse compared to FA maps: only a few rows/entries in the RA matrix have high norms while the majority are near zero, consistent with the qualitative analysis in Section 5.1 and Figure 2.

The execution outcomes confirm that the RA matrix R is lower triangular (causal), consistent with its derivation via element-wise multiplication with the lower-triangular forward attention A (Equation 17).
